ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'fruit juice drink' class; no lychee-specific juice drink entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code AN0KN covers fruit nectars and drinks broadly; no lychee-specific subcode exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for lychee juice drink as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of lychee juice drink is approximately 250 ml (one glass), providing about 75 kcal and 19 g of carbohydrates.

nutrient_source.title: Lychee juice drink — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id D03070019 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for lychee juice drink exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI 57 (medium) is estimated by analogy to mixed fruit juice drinks and lychee-based beverages documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (57 × 19.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 10.8. Input record carried GI 57 with confidence 0.5; this is retained as an analogous estimate pending direct measurement.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (5.5 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, despite low sodium (1 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid lychee juice drink because its added sugar load (5.5 g/100 g) independently raises blood pressure risk through sugar-driven insulin and sympathetic nervous system activation, even though sodium is negligible; replace with plain water or unsweetened beverages.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 5.5 g added sugar per 100 g, zero dietary fibre, and zero protein — liquid carbohydrate causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation per ADA guidelines.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid lychee juice drink entirely, as it is a sugar-sweetened beverage delivering rapidly absorbed liquid sugar (5.5 g/100 g, GI 57) with no fibre or protein to blunt the glycaemic response, causing sharp postprandial glucose spikes; choose water or unsweetened beverages instead.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and elevated CVD risk per AHA dietary guidelines.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit lychee juice drink because habitual sugar-sweetened beverage intake raises triglycerides and lowers HDL cholesterol; the 5.5 g added sugar per 100 g serving contributes to cardiometabolic risk and should be replaced with water or unsweetened drinks.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lychee juice drink is a commercially produced, sweetened fruit beverage made from lychee juice concentrate diluted with water and supplemented with added sugars or sweeteners. It is a clear to pale-pink liquid with a sweet, floral, and mildly tropical flavour characteristic of the lychee fruit (Litchi chinensis). The drink is ultra-processed and classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB). Per 100 g it provides 30 kcal, 7.58 g carbohydrate (of which 5.5 g is sugar), negligible protein (0 g), negligible fat (0 g), zero dietary fibre, and very low sodium (1 mg). The glycaemic index is estimated at 57 (medium), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 10.8 per 250 ml serving. Clinically, lychee juice drink is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes due to its rapidly absorbed liquid sugar and absence of fibre or protein to moderate glycaemic response. It is also of concern for hypertension because added sugar independently elevates blood pressure risk, and for cardiovascular disease because regular SSB consumption raises triglycerides and lowers HDL. The drink is vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is commonly consumed as a snack beverage or accompaniment to meals in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, and is widely available in canned, bottled, and tetra-pack formats.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lychee juice drink?
Is lychee drink high in sugar?
Is lychee drink suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id D03070019, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.5) pending direct measurement; all other fields are supported by approved sources.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo — this is a diluted juice drink with no protein-containing ingredients by formulation. fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo — no fat-containing ingredients are present in this beverage formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo — juice concentrate with no whole-fruit fibre retained. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero consistent with zero total fat. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 per HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this specific lychee juice drink product are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026. GI value of 57 is an analogous estimate based on Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); direct GI measurement for this product is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycaemic load calculated as (57 × 19.0 g carbohydrate per 250 g serving) / 100 = 10.8. This food is not a Singaporean dish requiring Chinese/Malay/Tamil/Hindi local names; it is a generic commercial beverage category record.
